chunks.title: Frontend / backend split
chunks.text: Web applications split into frontend code that runs in the browser (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) and backend code that runs on a server (Python, Node, Go, Java). The browser fetches the frontend, executes it, and the JS makes API calls to the backend over HTTP for data and actions. The backend reads/writes a database and returns JSON. This split scales — many users hit one backend; the backend can be replicated for more capacity.

chunks.title: HTML and the DOM
chunks.text: HTML describes the structure of a page using nested tags. The browser parses HTML into the DOM (Document Object Model), a tree of nodes JavaScript can read and modify. Semantic tags (header, nav, main, article, section, footer) carry meaning that helps accessibility tools and search engines. The viewport meta tag controls how mobile browsers render the page. Forms (input, textarea, select, button) submit data to the server.

chunks.title: CSS and layout
chunks.text: CSS styles HTML. Selectors target elements (.btn, #header, ul > li). The cascade resolves conflicting rules by specificity, source order, and !important. The box model gives each element content, padding, border, margin. Flexbox lays out items along one axis; CSS Grid handles two-dimensional layouts. Media queries adapt styles to viewport width for responsive design. Modern utility-first frameworks (Tailwind) compose small classes instead of writing custom CSS.

chunks.title: JavaScript and the browser
chunks.text: JavaScript runs in every browser. Modern JS (ES2015+) has let/const, arrow functions, destructuring, modules, promises, async/await. The event loop runs JS single-threaded, processing tasks from a queue — DOM events, timers, network callbacks. Async APIs (fetch, setTimeout) don't block; they schedule a callback for later. The DOM API (document.querySelector, addEventListener) lets JS read and modify the page. Frameworks (React, Vue, Svelte) abstract direct DOM manipulation.

chunks.title: REST APIs
chunks.text: REST is the dominant style for HTTP APIs. Resources are named with URLs (/users/42, /orders/99). HTTP verbs express actions: GET reads, POST creates, PUT/PATCH updates, DELETE removes. Responses use status codes (200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found, 500 Internal Server Error) and a JSON body. APIs should be stateless — each request carries everything needed to handle it. Authentication via tokens (JWT, OAuth) in the Authorization header.

chunks.title: Frontend frameworks
chunks.text: Modern UIs are built with component-based frameworks. React (Facebook) and Vue use virtual DOM diffing to update the page efficiently. Svelte compiles components to direct DOM operations with no runtime. Components hold state (useState in React, ref in Vue), receive props from parents, and re-render when state changes. Frameworks handle routing (React Router, Vue Router), forms, and component lifecycle. Build tools (Vite, webpack) bundle and optimize the code.

chunks.title: Backend frameworks and databases
chunks.text: Backend frameworks structure HTTP handlers, routing, middleware, and database access. Examples: Express (Node), Django/Flask/FastAPI (Python), Spring (Java), Rails (Ruby), Gin (Go). Persistence usually goes through an ORM (Sequelize, SQLAlchemy, Prisma) that maps objects to relational rows. Alternatively, raw SQL or NoSQL drivers. Connection pooling reuses database connections across requests. Migrations track schema changes alongside code in version control.

chunks.title: Deployment and hosting
chunks.text: A modern deployment uses a CDN to serve static assets (HTML, CSS, JS, images) close to the user, and a backend API hosted on a cloud provider. Domain names point at the CDN via DNS A or CNAME records. HTTPS certificates from Let's Encrypt secure the connection. Common hosts: Vercel and Netlify (static + serverless), AWS / GCP / Azure (full control), Render / Railway / Fly.io (managed PaaS). Containerized apps deploy via Docker and Kubernetes.

chunks.title: React hooks
chunks.text: Hooks let function components use state and side effects. useState gives a piece of state and a setter. useEffect runs side effects after render with control via the dependency array. useMemo memoizes expensive computations; useCallback memoizes callback references. useRef holds a mutable value that doesn't trigger re-render. useContext reads context. Custom hooks (any function calling other hooks) extract reusable logic. Rules: only call at the top level, only from React functions.

chunks.title: React context
chunks.text: Context provides a way to pass data through the component tree without prop drilling. Create with createContext, supply a value with a Provider, consume with useContext. Common uses: theme, current user, language, feature flags. Avoid overuse — every component reading the context re-renders when the value changes. For frequently-changing state, prefer state management libraries or split context to limit re-render scope.

chunks.title: State management
chunks.text: For state that spans many components, React's local state and context are often enough. For complex apps, Redux (predictable state, time-travel debugging) or Zustand (simpler, smaller bundle) or Jotai (atomic) help. For server state — data fetched from APIs — React Query / TanStack Query or SWR handle caching, refetching, optimistic updates, and stale-while-revalidate. Use the right tool per state type: local, global UI, server.

chunks.title: Client-side routing
chunks.text: Single-page applications route in the browser without full page reloads. React Router uses the history API to update the URL and render different components based on the path. Routes can be nested, parameterized (/users/:id), and protected (auth required). React Router 6's data APIs add server-style loaders and actions. For multi-page apps, Next.js does file-based routing with server-side rendering.

chunks.title: Server-side rendering (SSR)
chunks.text: SSR renders the React component tree to HTML on the server and ships that HTML to the browser. Benefits: faster initial paint, SEO (crawlers see content immediately), better social previews. The page is hydrated on the client — React attaches event handlers to existing DOM. Next.js and Remix make SSR turnkey. Trade-offs: server cost, more complex caching, harder to use browser-only APIs without guards.

chunks.title: Static site generation (SSG)
chunks.text: SSG pre-renders pages at build time, serving plain HTML at request time. Fast (no per-request rendering), cheap (static hosting), secure (no server logic). Right for blogs, marketing sites, documentation. Tools: Next.js (getStaticProps), Astro, Eleventy, Hugo, Jekyll. Incremental Static Regeneration revalidates pages periodically without rebuilding everything. SSG combines well with CDN edge caching.

chunks.title: Next.js fundamentals
chunks.text: Next.js is a React meta-framework. App Router uses React Server Components for component-level server rendering. File-system based routing (pages/ or app/ directory) maps file paths to URLs. API routes (app/api/) handle backend logic. Built-in image optimization, font optimization, ISR, edge runtime. Deploys on Vercel out of the box; self-hosting works on Node, Docker, or serverless. Dominant React framework as of 2024.

chunks.title: Vite
chunks.text: Vite is a modern build tool that serves modules over native ESM during development — zero bundling, near-instant hot reload. Production builds use Rollup for optimized bundles. Compared to webpack: dramatically faster cold start (seconds vs minutes for large projects), simpler config, first-class TypeScript and JSX. Vite is the default dev server for Vue, the Svelte ecosystem, and increasingly React with Vite-React templates.

chunks.title: TypeScript basics
chunks.text: TypeScript adds static types to JavaScript. Annotations on parameters, return types, and variables (let x: number) catch type errors at compile time. The type system supports generics, unions, intersections, structural typing, mapped types, conditional types. tsc compiles to JS; modern build tools strip types in place. Even without annotations, TypeScript's inference catches many bugs. Adoption has accelerated — most new JS projects start TypeScript.

chunks.title: ESLint and Prettier
chunks.text: ESLint catches bugs and enforces style on JavaScript/TypeScript. Plugins extend it for React, accessibility (jsx-a11y), TypeScript types, and security. Configured in eslint.config.js or .eslintrc. Prettier handles formatting (indent, quotes, line length) — no debate, no config arguments. The combo: Prettier formats, ESLint catches anti-patterns. Run both in CI with --max-warnings 0 to prevent regression.

chunks.title: WebSocket detail
chunks.text: WebSocket connections start as HTTP with an Upgrade header. After the handshake, both sides exchange framed messages over the same TCP connection. Browser API: new WebSocket(url), then onopen/onmessage/onclose/onerror handlers, and .send(data). Server libraries: ws (Node), websockets (Python), Gorilla (Go). Closed connections need application-level reconnection logic (often with exponential backoff).

chunks.title: GraphQL
chunks.text: GraphQL is a query language for APIs where the client declares exactly which fields it needs. The server defines a schema (types, queries, mutations, subscriptions); clients send queries; the server executes them and returns data shaped to the query. Eliminates over-fetching and under-fetching that plagues REST. Tools: Apollo Server/Client, urql, Relay. Trade-offs: caching is harder, monitoring is per-query, easy to write expensive queries.

chunks.title: gRPC
chunks.text: gRPC is an RPC framework using HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers (protobuf) for binary serialization. Schema-first: define services in .proto files, generate client/server code in many languages. Supports unary, server streaming, client streaming, and bidirectional streaming. Used heavily for internal microservices communication where binary efficiency and strong typing matter. gRPC-Web bridges browsers (limited streaming) to gRPC services.

chunks.title: Authentication patterns
chunks.text: Session-based auth stores session ID in a cookie; the server keeps a session table mapping IDs to users — simple, easy to revoke. Token-based auth (JWT) puts a signed token in the cookie or Authorization header — stateless, easy to scale, hard to revoke before expiry. OAuth 2.0 delegates auth to identity providers (Google, GitHub). Mix and match: short-lived JWTs + refresh tokens + revocation list balances security and scale.

chunks.title: CSRF and XSS in depth
chunks.text: XSS injects scripts into pages others view. Stored XSS persists (user submits, others see); reflected XSS comes from URL parameters; DOM-based XSS happens entirely client-side. Defenses: escape on output, Content Security Policy, HttpOnly cookies (so JS can't steal them). CSRF tricks an authenticated browser into making unwanted requests. Defenses: CSRF tokens, SameSite=Lax cookies (most browsers now default), checking the Origin header.

chunks.title: Web Workers
chunks.text: Web Workers run JavaScript in background threads, isolated from the main UI thread. They can't touch the DOM but they can do CPU-heavy work (data crunching, parsing) without blocking the interface. Communication via postMessage with structured cloning. Dedicated Workers serve one page; Shared Workers serve multiple. Service Workers are a specialized type used for offline support and background sync.

chunks.title: Service Workers and PWAs
chunks.text: A Service Worker is a script the browser runs in the background, separate from your page. It intercepts network requests, enabling offline support, push notifications, background sync. Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) combine service workers with a web app manifest to install on home screens, work offline, and feel native. Lighthouse audits PWA compliance. Used by Twitter Lite, Starbucks, many news sites.

chunks.title: Web accessibility (WCAG)
chunks.text: WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) defines how to make web content accessible to people with disabilities. Four principles: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust. Practical: semantic HTML, alt text on images, sufficient color contrast (4.5:1 for normal text), keyboard navigation works, focus indicators, ARIA attributes when semantic HTML isn't enough. Tools: axe DevTools, Lighthouse a11y audit, NVDA/VoiceOver for testing.

chunks.title: SEO fundamentals
chunks.text: SEO helps search engines understand and rank pages. On-page: descriptive titles, meta description, semantic HTML, structured data (JSON-LD), fast load times, mobile-friendly. Off-page: backlinks from reputable sites. Technical: clean URLs, canonical tags, sitemap.xml, robots.txt, server-rendered content for crawlers. Single-page apps need SSR or pre-rendering for SEO. Search Console reports indexing and ranking signals.

chunks.title: Core Web Vitals
chunks.text: Core Web Vitals are Google's metrics for real-user experience: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint, < 2.5s good) measures load speed; INP (Interaction to Next Paint, replaced FID; < 200ms good) measures responsiveness; CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift, < 0.1 good) measures visual stability. Lighthouse measures them in lab; Chrome User Experience Report has real user data. Affects search ranking.

chunks.title: Lazy loading
chunks.text: Lazy loading defers fetching resources until needed. Native: ,

chunks.title: Code splitting
chunks.text: Code splitting breaks the JS bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand. Vite and webpack split automatically on dynamic imports: const Comp = lazy(() => import('./Comp')). Splits by route, by component, or by user state. Reduces initial bundle size, improves time-to-interactive. Trade-off: more network requests on navigation; HTTP/2 multiplexing softens the hit.

chunks.title: WebAssembly
chunks.text: WebAssembly (Wasm) is a portable binary instruction format that runs near-native speed in browsers. Compiles from C, C++, Rust, Go, AssemblyScript. Used for performance-critical code: image processing, game engines, encoders, scientific computing. Figma uses Wasm for its design tools, AutoCAD for the web, Photoshop online. Wasm can call JavaScript and vice versa via JS bindings.

chunks.title: Browser caching
chunks.text: HTTP caching uses headers. Cache-Control: max-age=3600 (cache for an hour). Cache-Control: immutable (never recheck) for content-hashed assets. ETag and Last-Modified enable conditional requests (304 Not Modified saves bandwidth). The service worker cache offers programmatic control: precache the shell, runtime-cache API responses. Aggressive caching is the biggest perceived performance win.

chunks.title: CORS
chunks.text: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is the browser's policy preventing pages from making cross-origin requests without permission. Servers opt in by sending Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers. Preflight OPTIONS requests check whether the actual request will be allowed. Common errors: missing CORS headers, mismatched origin, preflight failing on custom headers. CORS only restricts browsers; server-to-server has no such restriction.

chunks.title: HTTP/2 server push
chunks.text: HTTP/2 server push lets a server send resources before the client asks — push the CSS and JS along with the HTML. In practice, it underperformed expectations: browser cache awareness was poor, the gains were marginal, and Chrome removed it in 2022. The lesson: 103 Early Hints is the replacement, telling the browser what to fetch while the server is still preparing the main response.
